description: Manages the lifecycle of applications, activity stacks, process creation, and core system navigation.
A native Android API that allows allocation and direct sharing of hardware memory buffers across different processes (e.g., camera to GPU).
General baseline Android operating system framework logs.
The virtual machine environment (ART) executing the application’s compiled Java/Kotlin bytecode.
Your specific mobile application package. Logs here originate directly from your unique business logic.
A popular Flutter package used to create, manage, and display localized push and system notifications.
Android’s Buffer Layer Architecture System Tracking queue, optimizing how graphic frames are passed to the compositor.
The engine side (like SurfaceFlinger) that consumes graphical frames produced by the app for display rendering.
Coordinates the timing of animations, input, and drawing. It synchronizes rendering with the display refresh rate to prevent tearing.
Logs related to database interactions (SQLite, Room, Hive, or local app caching engines).
Likely a highly specific local debug tag, process alias, or proprietary third-party framework identifier.
Google Firebase telemetry component tracking application user sessions, app launches, and engagement metrics.
Internal Flutter Firebase context provider tracking framework lifecycle states.
Standard infrastructure logs coming directly from the main Flutter framework engine.
Handles binding Android Activity/Fragment lifecycles to the Flutter engine view.
Plugin framework responsible for communicating with Android's GPS and network location services.
Java Native Interface logic connecting the C++ based Flutter engine to Android's native Java/Kotlin APIs.
The engine sub-component responsible for taking Flutter widget layers and drawing them onto the screen canvas.
A performance warning flag. Logs here mean a frame took too long to render, causing visible stutter (jank) for the user.
The Graphics Memory Allocator Hardware Abstraction Layer. It allocates raw memory on behalf of device display drivers.
Extended functionality hooks and allocations tied to the primary Gralloc hardware framework.
A high-level system service allocating system memory arrays for graphic and rendering layers.
Android’s hardware-accelerated 2D rendering engine. It handles drawing Android-native views using the device GPU.
High-importance or severe framework-level warnings flagged explicitly as crucial.
Deep engine logs concerning Flutter’s modern rendering backend (Impeller) processing via the Vulkan (Vk) graphics API on Android.
The Android system component responsible for intercepting and routing physical touch, gestures, and hardware key presses to your active screen.
Manages scheduled background tasks and deferred system processes via JobScheduler.
Android’s system-wide cryptographic storage daemon used to secure app keys, biometrics, and credentials safely.
Standard C library implementation used for basic system calls, memory management, and low-level execution.
A proprietary graphics optimization/vendor library frequently bundled within specific vendor chipsets (e.g., MediaTek display drivers).
Standard diagnostic logs generated by explicit print statements or simple debugging flags in code.
Android’s lmdkd daemon. If logs peak here, the OS is aggressively killing background services because the system is critically out of RAM.
The native Android multimedia framework handling audio, video playback, and codec operations.
Ensures backward compatibility for media playback controls, transport buttons, and lock-screen status tracking.
The internal system utility responsible for finding and loading shared native (.so) binary libraries.
Telemetry and hooks related to CPU performance monitoring, system tracing, or optimization tracking.
Manages application processes, lifecycle priorities, and standard process communication tools.
Optimizes initial application performance by installing profile-guided optimization files (PGO/Baseline Profiles).
A background data transport layer (often linked to Google Play Services) used to queue and upload crash reports and metrics.
A Flutter plugin utilized to kick intents out to the OS, such as launching an external web browser, dialing a phone number, or opening an email app.
Handles vendor-specific screen extensions, multi-window configurations, and foldable device screen form factors.
System service that governs screen layout, window layering, visibility states, and how different applications stack visually.
Internal window manager scheduler handling display, frame, or structural updates.
The base preloaded process from which all Android applications fork when launched, designed to drastically decrease application startup times.
